title: Orbital Mechanics Inversion (Lambert)
source_data_quality_note: Source MD is a widefield-fluorescence fan-out artifact; principle grounded in domain literature.

E.description: Orbital Mechanics Inversion (Lambert): Lambert problem: find velocity vectors that connect two position vectors in a fixed time-of-flight (orbital transfer). The forward operator produces the measurement through a 3-node primitive DAG (M.lambert.problem_solver…); recovery is posed as a nonlinear_inverse problem. Difficulty tier delta=3 with effective condition number kappa_eff~200; position_observation_error_km, perturbation_from_central_body set the accuracy floor at the Omega boundary. See the forward_model field for the closed-form equation.
E.forward_model: r1, r2 given, TOF = T; find v1 such that propagating from (r1,v1) reaches r2 in time T
E.world_state_x: initial and final velocity vectors v1, v2 defining the transfer orbit
E.observation_y: two position vectors r1, r2 and transfer time TOF

W.regime: Existence of the recovered orbital_elements_vector is guaranteed within the declared Omega bounds. Uniqueness holds on the measurement-supported subspace; out-of-support modes are controlled by declared priors. Stability is conditionally stable (kappa_eff ~= 200); position_observation_error_km dominates the stability cliff; the remaining mismatch parameters contribute higher-order bias terms. Observation gaussian sets the irreducible data-fidelity floor.

C.error_bound: Data-fidelity bound: Lambert solution accurate to < 1 m/s for 2-body problem. Noise floor: O(sigma / sqrt(kappa_eff)) with kappa_eff~200. Mismatch-induced bias + C2 * |position_observation_error_km|; iterative solvers achieve q=2 convergence (O(1/k^2)) near the optimum; epsilon_fn captures quality degradation across declared Omega.
C.complexity: O(N_iter) per Lambert solution (typically < 20 iterations) per iteration
